concept: Using linear classifiers on generated samples, we can find vectors corresponding to age, smile, or gender, enabling controlled modification.
title: Finding Directions in Latent Space

options.text: A starting point in latent space that the generator turns into an image
A physical storage drive where training photos are saved
A mathematical function that deletes low-quality generated faces

exercises.explain: GANs map random input points (latent vectors) to realistic output images, but finding the exact direction for a specific trait requires searching this space.
exercises.prompt: Before we find directions, what is a latent vector?

cards.body: Imagine having a knob that lets you make a generated face older, happier, or add glasses instantly. By studying the latent space of GANs, we can find these exact dials!
cards.title: Steering the Generator
What are Latent Directions?
cards.body: Latent space directions are vectors (paths) in the random noise space of a GAN. Moving along these vectors systematically changes specific attributes (like adding a smile) in the generated image.

cards.title: The Latent Compass
cards.body: Think of latent space like a giant map of a city. Moving North might make houses taller, while walking East might change their color. By finding the compass direction, we don't have to wander randomly.

By training a simple boundary line (linear classifier) on images with and without sunglasses, we find a vector pointing toward 'sunglasses'. Adding this vector to a face's vector adds sunglasses to the output face!
cards.title: Adding Glasses with Math

cards.body: You might think changing one trait changes everything else too. Actually, if we find orthogonal (independent) paths, we can add a smile without changing the person's age or hair color!
cards.title: Does Everything Change?
Before and After Direction Shift
cards.body: Compare how modifying a latent vector changes the output face in a controlled way.

exercises.prompt: How do researchers find the specific vectors for traits like age or smiles?
exercises.explain: We train simple classifiers on generated images to label them (e.g., 'smiling' vs 'not smiling'), then look at which direction in latent space separates these groups.

options.text: By training classifiers on generated images to find boundary lines in the latent space
By manually drawing pixels on the final generated images
By retraining the entire GAN from scratch for every single photo

exercises.prompt: True or False: We can modify a specific trait (like adding glasses) by adding a directional vector to the latent code.
exercises.explain: Moving a vector in a specific direction alters only the features associated with that direction, leaving others mostly unchanged.
A linear classifier finds a boundary in the latent space that separates images with a trait from those without it.
exercises.sentence: To find a trait vector, we look for a boundary line inside the [blank] space.
exercises.prompt: To find a trait vector, we look for a boundary line inside the _______ space.

options.text: Taking a generated face vector and adding the 'smile' vector to slowly transition its expression
Using Photoshop to copy and paste a smile onto a photograph

exercises.explain: Using a found direction vector to slowly slide a face from sad to happy is an application of latent space manipulation.
exercises.prompt: Which of these shows latent space direction manipulation?

options.text: They represent direction paths in latent space that correspond to specific physical features
They are the pixels that form the borders of the generated image
They are the error rates of the discriminator network

exercises.explain: Using linear classifiers lets us identify the vector direction of traits, allowing us to control features like age or expression. Up next, we will explore conditional GANs to guide generation from the start!
exercises.prompt: What are latent direction vectors in a generative model?
